stem: Use Lagrange multipliers to minimize \( f(x, y) = x^{2} + y^{2} \) subject to the constraint \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y = [[c]] \). What is the minimum value of \( f \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the constraint value \( [[c]] \), not the minimized \( x^{2}+y^{2} \).
This is \( a^{2}+b^{2} \), the denominator, without accounting for the size of the constraint.
This doubles the correct minimum value.
This ignores the coefficients of the constraint entirely.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Use Lagrange multipliers to find the maximum value of \( f(x, y) = xy \) subject to the constraint \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y = [[k]] \).
solution: Setting \( \nabla f = \lambda \nabla g \) gives \( y = \lambda([[a]]) \) and \( x = \lambda([[b]]) \), so \( x/[[b]] = y/[[a]] \). Substituting \( x = \frac{[[b]]\cdot[[k]]}{2\cdot[[a]]\cdot[[b]]} = \frac{[[k]]}{2([[a]])} \) and \( y = \frac{[[k]]}{2([[b]])} \) into the constraint and simplifying gives \( f = xy = \dfrac{[[k]]^{2}}{4([[a]])([[b]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses a denominator of \( 2ab \) instead of \( 4ab \).
This forgets the factor of 4 in the denominator entirely.
This forgets to square \( [[k]] \) in the numerator.
This ignores \( [[k]] \) and returns just \( ab \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Use Lagrange multipliers to minimize \( f(x, y, z) = x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2} \) subject to the constraint \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y + [[c]]z = [[d]] \). What is the minimum value of \( f \)?
solution: Setting \( \nabla f = \lambda \nabla g \) gives \( 2x = \lambda([[a]]) \), \( 2y = \lambda([[b]]) \), \( 2z = \lambda([[c]]) \), so each coordinate is proportional to its constraint coefficient. Substituting into the constraint gives \( f = \dfrac{[[d]]^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}+[[c]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the constraint value \( [[d]] \), not the minimized \( x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2} \).
This is \( a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}\), the denominator, without accounting for the size of the constraint.
This doubles the correct minimum value.
This ignores the coefficients of the constraint entirely.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Use Lagrange multipliers to find the maximum value of \( f(x, y) = xy \) subject to the constraint \( x^{2} + y^{2} = [[r]]^{2} \).
solution: Setting \( \nabla f = \lambda \nabla g \) gives \( y = 2\lambda x \) and \( x = 2\lambda y \), so \( x^{2} = y^{2} \). On the constraint \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[r]]^{2} \) this forces \( x^{2}=y^{2}=\tfrac{[[r]]^{2}}{2} \), and the maximum of \( xy \) occurs where \( x \) and \( y \) have the same sign, giving \( f = xy = \dfrac{[[r]]^{2}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 after solving \( x^{2}=y^{2}=r^{2}/2 \).
This uses a denominator of 4 instead of 2.
This is the MINIMUM value of \( xy \) on the circle (where \( x \) and \( y \) have opposite signs), not the maximum.
This forgets to square \( [[r]] \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Use Lagrange multipliers to find the minimum value of \( f(x, y) = x + y \) subject to the constraint \( xy = [[k]] \), with \( x > 0 \) and \( y > 0 \).
solution: Setting \( \nabla f = \lambda \nabla g \) gives \( 1 = \lambda y \) and \( 1 = \lambda x \), so \( x = y \). On the constraint \( xy=[[k]] \) this forces \( x=y=\sqrt{[[k]]}=[[s]] \), so the minimum value is \( f = x+y = 2([[s]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the constraint value \( [[k]] \), not the minimized sum \( x+y \).
This is only one of the two equal coordinates \( x=y=[[s]] \), not their sum.
This is \( x^{2} \) (or \( [[k]] \) again in disguise), not \( x+y \).
This halves the constraint value instead of taking \( 2\sqrt{[[k]]} \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A closed rectangular box with dimensions \( x \), \( y \), \( z \) (in cm) must have volume \( xyz = [[v]] \) cubic cm. Using Lagrange multipliers to minimize the total surface area \( S(x,y,z) = 2(xy + yz + zx) \) subject to this volume constraint, what is the minimum possible surface area (in square cm)?
solution: Setting \( \nabla S = \lambda \nabla(xyz) \) gives \( y+z=\lambda yz \), \( x+z=\lambda xz \), \( x+y=\lambda xy \); by symmetry the minimizer is the CUBE \( x=y=z=[[e]] \) (since \( [[e]]^{3}=[[v]] \)). There \( S = 2(3\cdot[[e]]^{2}) = 6([[e]])^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the volume \( [[v]] \), not the surface area.
This forgets the factor of 2 in \( S=2(xy+yz+zx) \).
This uses only 2 of the 3 face-pairs instead of all 3.
This uses the edge length linearly instead of squaring it.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Use Lagrange multipliers (with two multipliers, one per constraint) to minimize \( f(x, y, z) = x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2} \) subject to BOTH \( x + y + z = [[k]] \) and \( x - y = [[m]] \) (the intersection of two planes, a line). What is the minimum value of \( f \)?
solution:
Setting \( 
abla f = \lambda
abla(x+y+z) + \mu
abla(x-y) \) gives \( 2x=\lambda+\mu \), \( 2y=\lambda-\mu \), \( 2z=\lambda \). Solving this together with the two constraints \( x+y+z=[[k]] \) and \( x-y=[[m]] \) gives the unique closest point on the line to the origin, and \( f = x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2} = [[answer]] \) there.
distractor_rationales: This simply adds the squares of the two constraint constants, ignoring how they interact geometrically.
This halves the naive sum of squared constants instead of solving the actual Lagrange system.
This divides only the first constraint's square by 3 (as if it were the sole constraint on 3 equal coordinates), ignoring the second constraint.
This only averages the contribution of the second constraint, not both.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Minimize \( f(x, y) = x^{2} + y^{2} \) subject to \( [[a]]x + [[b]]y = [[c]] \). The Lagrange condition \( \nabla f = \lambda \nabla g \) introduces a multiplier \( \lambda \), which also equals the rate of change of the minimum value of \( f \) with respect to the constraint constant \( [[c]] \) (the envelope theorem). What is the value of \( \lambda \) at the optimum?
solution: Setting \( 2x=\lambda [[a]] \), \( 2y=\lambda [[b]] \) gives \( x=\lambda [[a]]/2 \), \( y=\lambda [[b]]/2 \). Substituting into \( [[a]]x+[[b]]y=[[c]] \) gives \( \lambda\dfrac{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}}{2}=[[c]] \), so \( \lambda = \dfrac{2\cdot[[c]]}{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that comes from \( f_{x}=2x \) and \( f_{y}=2y \).
This squares the constraint constant \( [[c]] \) instead of using it linearly.
This uses \( a+b \) instead of \( a^{2}+b^{2} \) in the denominator.
This returns the denominator \( a^{2}+b^{2} \) itself instead of \( \lambda \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
